subsystem: HTTP request-header reader for a MAPI/groupware server. Http::HrReadHeaders() reads the request line and header lines off a network channel, splits each 'Name: value' line, folds RFC-822 continuation lines, stores them in a std::map, and then hands off to HrParseHeaders(). This is the front-door parser that turns raw client bytes into structured headers before any authentication.
provenance: Identified by the Zarafa/Kopano MAPI conventions: the HRESULT return type, the hrSuccess sentinel, MAPI_E_TOO_BIG, the ec_log_debug / hr_ldebug logging macros, the m_lpChannel->HrReadLine channel abstraction, and the Http:: class scope with m_strAction / mapHeaders members. The in-file banner names corpus/train/train_0110_cwe770.cpp and the leading comment '/* sample: primevul_validation_0319_fixed */'. The cwe770 filename and the newly-added MAX_HEADER_LENGTH cap indicate this is the resource-throttling-hardened variant of a Kopano HTTP header reader.
trust_boundary: Every byte of strBuffer originates from m_lpChannel->HrReadLine() (line 14), i.e. the remote HTTP client, and is fully attacker-controlled. This runs while reading request headers, BEFORE authentication, so all inputs are unauthenticated and hostile. The header line contents, their length, their count, the presence/position of the ':' delimiter, the leading whitespace of continuation lines, and whether a line contains the substring 'Authorization' are all chosen by the attacker. Only strBuffer.empty() (end of headers) and the HrReadLine HRESULT are trusted signals.

what_the_code_does.explanation: Member function of the Http class returning an HRESULT. Locals: hr holds channel/parse status; strBuffer receives one line at a time; n counts lines so line 0 is treated as the request/action line and the rest as headers; iHeader is an iterator into the member map mapHeaders (std::map<string,string>) initialised to end() so a continuation line arriving before any real header can be detected; MAX_HEADER_LENGTH = 65536 is the cumulative header-size cap (the CWE-770 throttling control); numOfBytesRead accumulates the total bytes read across all lines to enforce that cap.

Logs a debug banner and enters a do/while loop. Each iteration pulls one line from the channel into strBuffer via HrReadLine. On any read error the function returns hr immediately. An empty line (strBuffer.empty()) marks the CRLF that terminates the header block, so the loop breaks. Because of this empty-check, every later access to strBuffer[0] is guaranteed to have at least one character.

The running total of header bytes is increased by the current line's length and, if the cumulative total exceeds 65536, the request is rejected with MAPI_E_TOO_BIG. This is the resource-throttling guard that bounds total memory/CPU spent accumulating headers and prevents an attacker from streaming unbounded header data (the CWE-770 fix). numOfBytesRead and MAX_HEADER_LENGTH are both size_t so the comparison and addition are unsigned and, given the 64 KiB cap, do not realistically overflow.

For the first non-empty line (n == 0) the whole line is stored verbatim as m_strAction, i.e. the HTTP request/method line (e.g. 'GET /path HTTP/1.1'). It is not parsed here. Every subsequent line falls into the else branch and is treated as a header or a continuation line.

For a header line, pos is the index of the first ':' delimiter, or std::string::npos (SIZE_MAX) if the line contains no colon at all. start is an index used to skip leading whitespace on continuation lines. Note that pos is used later for arithmetic (pos + 2) and as a substring position without any check that a colon was actually found.

If the line begins with SP or TAB it is an RFC-822 folded continuation of the previous header. If no previous header exists (iHeader == end()) the line is skipped with continue. Otherwise leading whitespace is consumed by advancing start, then the remainder (strBuffer.substr(start)) is appended to the previous header's value via the saved iterator iHeader->second. std::map iterators are node-based and remain valid across the emplace() insertions done elsewhere in the loop, so iHeader is not dangling. The while loop that advances start is bounded because std::string::operator[](size()) legally returns a reference to the terminating '\0', which is neither SP nor TAB, so the loop stops at end-of-string even if the whole line is whitespace. (Note: the actual token above is iHeader->second; the '->' is preserved verbatim.)

For a fresh header line the name is strBuffer.substr(0, pos) (everything before the colon) and the value is strBuffer.substr(pos + 2), which assumes the delimiter is exactly ': ' (colon followed by one space) and skips both characters. The pair is inserted with emplace(); if the header name already exists emplace is a no-op and r.first points at the existing element. iHeader is updated to the (new or existing) element so a following continuation line can append to it. This line is the critical one: pos comes directly from find(':') with no validation, and 'pos + 2' plus substr(pos + 2) is unsafe when the colon is missing or is the final character. (The saved iterator token is iHeader = r.first.)

Debug logging of each header. If the line contains the substring 'Authorization' anywhere, a redacted placeholder is logged to avoid writing credentials to the debug log; otherwise the raw line is logged. n is then incremented. The redaction is a substring test on the whole line, not a parse of the header name, which makes it both over- and under-inclusive (see risks).

The loop continues as long as reads succeed; it exits on an empty line (break), a read error (early return), or the size cap (early return). After the header block is read, HrParseHeaders() interprets the collected map, its failure is logged at debug level via hr_ldebug, and the resulting hr is returned. The downstream HrParseHeaders body is not shown and is out of scope for this file.

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: pos = strBuffer.find(':') is used as strBuffer.substr(pos + 2) with no check that the colon exists or that two more characters follow it. std::string::substr(pos) throws std::out_of_range when its position argument exceeds size(). If an attacker sends a header line whose ONLY colon is the last character, e.g. the line 'X:' (size 2, colon at index 1), then pos + 2 == 3 > size() == 2 and substr(3) throws std::out_of_range. This happens on unauthenticated input, inside the pre-auth header read. Unless a caller higher up wraps this in a try/catch, the exception propagates and terminates the request handler / worker, giving a trivially reproducible remote denial of service from a single crafted header line. The implementation must validate that a colon was found AND that there are enough trailing characters (or clamp with std::min / check pos != npos && pos + 2 <= size()) before calling substr(pos + 2).

When the header line contains NO colon at all, find(':') returns std::string::npos == SIZE_MAX. The expression pos + 2 then wraps around (SIZE_MAX + 2 == 1) rather than throwing, so substr(pos + 2) silently becomes substr(1): the code inserts a bogus header whose name is the entire line (substr(0, npos)) and whose value is the line minus its first character. This is an unsigned-integer wrap (CWE-191) that turns malformed input into a corrupted map entry instead of a clean rejection, and it depends on the accident that npos+2 lands on a valid index; a different constant could turn the same pattern into an out-of-range throw. The implementation must reject/skip lines where pos == npos before doing any pos-relative arithmetic.

The credential-redaction gate is a plain substring search over the whole line, not a check of the parsed header name. A folded continuation line of an Authorization header (a line that starts with SP/TAB and carries only the credential material, with the name 'Authorization' on the previous line) does NOT contain the substring 'Authorization', so it falls to the else branch and the raw secret is written verbatim into the debug log (CWE-532, cleartext credentials in log). Conversely, any unrelated header whose value merely mentions 'Authorization' is needlessly redacted. The safe contract is to redact based on the parsed header name (case-insensitively) and to carry the redaction state across folded continuation lines.

Even when a colon exists and does not throw, substr(pos + 2) hard-codes the assumption that the delimiter is exactly ': ' (colon + single space). HTTP permits zero or more OWS after the colon, and RFC 7230 allows 'Name:value'. For 'X:value' (no space) pos == 1 and substr(3) drops the leading 'v', storing 'alue'; for multiple spaces the value keeps the extra leading whitespace. This is a parsing-correctness / input-validation defect (CWE-20) that silently mangles header values; downstream security decisions (Host, Content-Length, auth scheme) made on a corrupted value can be wrong. The implementation should skip exactly the colon and then trim optional whitespace, not blindly advance by two.

The 65536-byte cumulative cap is the resource-throttling control and is correctly enforced across the whole header block, which is the CWE-770 hardening — this is a positive. Two residual notes: (1) the cap is checked AFTER the full line has already been read into strBuffer and added, so the peak memory of a single line is bounded only by whatever per-line limit HrReadLine itself enforces, not by this function; if HrReadLine has no line-length limit, one enormous line could momentarily allocate far more than 64 KiB before the check fires. (2) The number of distinct headers (map entries) is bounded only indirectly by the byte cap, which is adequate. The implementation should confirm HrReadLine imposes its own per-line bound.

summary: The CWE-770 hardening (the 64 KiB MAX_HEADER_LENGTH cap) is present and correct, but the header splitter has a separate, pre-authentication defect: strBuffer.substr(pos + 2) at line 40 uses the result of find(':') without validating it, so a header line ending in a colon throws an uncaught std::out_of_range (remote DoS) and a colonless line triggers an unsigned wrap (npos + 2 == 1) that corrupts the parse. A secondary issue: the Authorization redaction is a whole-line substring test, so folded continuation lines can leak credentials into the debug log.

reviewed_anchors.reason: The cumulative header-size cap; the CWE-770 throttling control. Correctly enforced at lines 20-23; adequate as a resource limit.
size_t accumulator bounded by the 64 KiB cap; no realistic unsigned overflow at these magnitudes.
Only distinguishes request line (n==0) from headers; monotonic, no bound-sensitive use.
std::map node iterators stay valid across emplace(); end()-init guards use before any header exists — no use-after-free / invalidation.
Ensures strBuffer[0] accesses (line 31) are in-bounds; correct guard.
Safe because the empty-line break at line 17-18 guarantees at least one character.
operator[](size()) returns the '\0' terminator (not SP/TAB), so the loop terminates at end-of-string; no out-of-bounds walk.
Result used unvalidated in pos+2 arithmetic and substr — source of the throw and the unsigned wrap.
Throws std::out_of_range on a line ending in ':' (DoS); wraps to substr(1) when pos==npos; and hard-codes ': ' delimiter (mis-parse).
substr(0, npos/any) never throws for the position=0 form; header name is at worst the whole line — a correctness effect of the same colonless-line bug, covered by the pos finding.
Duplicate header names are dropped (emplace no-op) and iHeader still points at the existing element; a policy quirk, not a memory-safety issue.
The untrusted-input source; per-line length bounding is the channel's contract (see residual note), not this function's memory bug.
Whole-line substring test misses folded Authorization continuation lines -> cleartext credential in debug log (CWE-532).
Downstream consumer whose body is not in this file; out of scope.

findings.title: Unvalidated find(':') result -> uncaught std::out_of_range in substr(pos + 2) (remote DoS)
findings.bug_class: Uncaught exception from unvalidated substring position (missing input validation)
findings.invariant: Before calling substr(pos + 2), the code must verify pos != std::string::npos AND pos + 2 <= strBuffer.size() (or clamp with std::min), so the substring position can never exceed the string length.
findings.failure_mode: A crafted, unauthenticated header line whose only ':' is the final character (e.g. 'X:', size 2, colon at index 1) makes pos + 2 == 3 > size() == 2; std::string::substr(3) throws std::out_of_range. Reading headers happens before authentication, so a single line crashes the request handler; absent an upstream try/catch the exception propagates and terminates the worker/connection, a reproducible remote denial of service.
findings.what_to_confirm: Whether any caller of HrReadHeaders wraps it in a try/catch; if not, confirm the process/worker aborts on a header line ending in ':' and add a pos-validity + trailing-length check before substr.

findings.title: Unsigned wrap: pos + 2 with find(':')==npos silently yields substr(1) and corrupts the parse
findings.bug_class: Unsigned integer wraparound in substring offset
findings.invariant: A colonless header line must be rejected or skipped before any pos-relative arithmetic; pos == npos must never flow into pos + N.
findings.failure_mode: For a header line with no ':', find returns npos == SIZE_MAX; pos + 2 wraps to 1, so the value becomes substr(1) and the name becomes the entire line (substr(0, npos)). Malformed input is stored as a corrupted header rather than rejected, and the code only avoids a throw here by the coincidence that SIZE_MAX + 2 lands on a valid index.
findings.what_to_confirm: Send a header line with no colon and confirm a bogus map entry is created (name == whole line, value == line minus first char) instead of the line being skipped.

findings.title: Authorization redaction misses folded continuation lines -> credentials in debug log
findings.bug_class: Insertion of sensitive information into log file
findings.invariant: Redaction must be driven by the parsed header name (case-insensitive) and must persist across RFC-822 folded continuation lines that belong to a sensitive header.
findings.failure_mode: The redaction is a whole-line substring test. If the client folds an Authorization header so the credential material sits on a following line beginning with SP/TAB, that continuation line does not contain the string 'Authorization' and is logged verbatim at debug level, leaking the credential to the log. (It also over-redacts unrelated headers whose values merely contain 'Authorization'.)
findings.what_to_confirm: With debug logging enabled, send a folded Authorization header and confirm whether the credential-bearing continuation line is written to the log in cleartext.

audit_checklist: Confirm whether HrReadHeaders (or its callers) is wrapped in try/catch; a header line ending in ':' makes substr(pos+2) throw std::out_of_range (line 40) — verify it does not abort the worker.
Add a guard at line 40: reject/skip when pos == std::string::npos, and require pos + 2 <= strBuffer.size() before substr(pos + 2); do not rely on npos+2 wrapping to a valid index.
Replace the fixed ': ' assumption (substr(pos + 2)) with 'skip the colon then trim optional whitespace' so 'Name:value' and multi-space forms parse correctly (line 40).
Drive the Authorization redaction from the parsed header name and carry the redaction state across folded continuation lines so credentials cannot reach the debug log (lines 45-48).
Verify m_lpChannel->HrReadLine enforces its own per-line length limit; the MAX_HEADER_LENGTH cap (lines 20-23) bounds cumulative bytes but only after a full line has been read into strBuffer.
Confirm the 65536 cumulative cap is the intended policy and that rejecting with MAPI_E_TOO_BIG cleanly aborts the request without leaving partial state.
